title: Having reached the $2 million target, I hereby announce my official retirement.
author: Ok-Goat-8851
selftext:
Woke up this morning and saw my account finally cross the $2 million mark. That was the exact number I set for myself when I first went full-time. Now that I’ve hit it, I’m officially calling it a career today. These 25 years of ups and downs were anything but easy, and now I’m ready to step away and actually enjoy life.



Before I go, I wanted to lay out the core strategy I’ve used. Hopefully, it’ll give some of you the breakthrough you’ve been looking for.



Pic 1: Forget the noise in the middle; it’s just a trap. You only need to focus on two levels: the Previous Day High (PDH) and the Previous Day Low (PDL). These lines are where retail traders stack their stop losses. The market is basically a massive liquidity-eating machine, constantly hunting for those stop-loss pools to fill big institutional orders.



Pic 2: When you see the price creeping above the PDH, don’t get excited. That’s a fakeout—a Liquidity Sweep. It pierces the high to bait breakout traders into buying, while also hitting the stops of early short sellers (which are also buy orders). All that sudden buying pressure gives institutions the liquidity they need to dump their longs and build massive short positions the other way.



Pic 3: The only real proof you need to go short is a Market Structure Shift (MSS). The price has to drop off a cliff and snap through the nearest support level. This violent plunge rips open a Fair Value Gap (FVG)—the void left behind by the institutions. That gap is their only ticket back into the fight.



Pic 4: Whatever you do, don't chase the price. After the structure breaks, the market will make a weak little bounce. The exact second it drills back into that FVG, that’s your sniper entry. By getting in there, your stop loss is safe right above the sweep high, and you’re risking very little for a massive, violent drop.



Pic 5: Once you’re in, keep your eyes on one target: the Previous Day Low (PDL). Since the buy-side liquidity at the top was already swallowed whole, the market is naturally going to gravitate toward the stop-loss pool at the bottom.



Summary: No sweep, no trade. In the world of ICT, every breakout is just a liquidity trap. You aren’t trading price; you’re trading retail despair. Sweep the high, wait for the retrace, and kill it at the low. It’s that simple, and that brutal.



Hope you guys hit your goals soon and get out there to enjoy life. Cheers!

comments.author: WinterTiny1563
comments.body: Man, I wish I saw this 4 hours ago. I literally just blew 20% of my small account trying to buy the breakout above yesterday's high. I thought it was going to the moon, but it reversed so fast I didn't even have time to react. I’m 3 years into this and still feel like a total exit liquidity for the big guys. Honestly, I'm at the point where I want to just give up. Is it always this brutal?

replies.author: Ok-Goat-8851
replies.body: Don't beat yourself up too much. We’ve all been there. The market is designed to make that 'breakout' look like a golden opportunity—that’s how they bait you. 3 years is actually the 'danger zone' where you know enough to be confident but not enough to be disciplined

comments.author: Dark_Melon23
comments.body: can anyone tell me why downvotes on the post? Like the strat that bad or?
